flashcards.front: What triggers the execution of the Grandparent constructor when creating a Child object?
flashcards.back: The implicit super() call in the Parent constructor, which is invoked by the Child's implicit super() call.

flashcards.front: When do instance initializer blocks run in the Child class?
flashcards.back: After all superclass constructors (Grandparent and Parent) have completed, but before the Child's own constructor body executes.

flashcards.front: Why does the Child constructor execute last in the hierarchy?
flashcards.back: Because subclass constructors (Child) must fully initialize their superclass (Parent and Grandparent) before initializing their own state.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the implicit super() call in constructors?
flashcards.back: It ensures that the superclass's constructors are executed before the subclass's constructors, maintaining proper initialization order.

flashcards.front: What happens if a subclass constructor does NOT call super()?
flashcards.back: The compiler inserts an implicit super() call to the no-arg superclass constructor. If none exists, a compile-time error occurs.
